# Quick Start Guide
## ⚡ 30-Minute Fast Track
This quick start guide helps you navigate the documentation collection and begin working with DITA immediately.

Step 1: Understand What DITA Is (10 minutes)
Read: DITA Quick Introduction Guide
Key takeaways:
- DITA = structured XML content that's reusable and multi-format
- Topics are small, focused information units
- Maps assemble topics into deliverables
- Publishing happens through DITA-OT
Skip to: Section 2 (DITA mental model) and Section 4 (most important features)
Step 2: See a Working Example (10 minutes)
Explore: the examples/dita-html5-sidebar-starter-v2/ directory in the starter repo
Actions:
- Look at sample topics in dita/topics/
- Open the map in dita/maps/getting-started.ditamap
- Review the plug-in structure in plugins/
What you'll learn:
- What a DITA topic looks like
- How maps organize topics
- Basic project structure
Step 3: Try Your First Build (10 minutes)
Prerequisites: DITA-OT 4.x installed (dita-ot.org)
Build command:
```bash
cd examples/dita-html5-sidebar-starter-v2
./build/build.sh  # or build.ps1 on Windows
```
View output: Open out/html/index.html in browser
What you'll learn:
- How to run a DITA build
- What HTML output looks like
- Where files are generated

text:
# Quick Start Guide
## 🔍 Common Questions Answered
This quick start guide helps you navigate the documentation collection and begin working with DITA immediately.

"What's the difference between DITA and Markdown/Word/etc?"
Answer: Getting Started → Section 1
DITA is structured, semantic, and designed for:
- Content reuse at scale
- Multiple output formats from one source
- Product/platform/audience variants
- Long-lived documentation with frequent updates
"How do I know if my team needs DITA?"
Answer: DITA Overview → When to use DITA
Consider DITA if you have:
- ✅ Multiple products/variants needing shared content
- ✅ Multiple output formats (HTML + PDF + help)
- ✅ Large documentation sets (100+ topics)
- ✅ Frequent releases and updates
- ✅ Multiple writers needing consistency
Skip DITA if you have:
- ❌ Single product, single format
- ❌ Small doc set (<20 topics)
- ❌ Infrequent updates
- ❌ No reuse requirements
"How long does it take to learn DITA?"
Answer: Depends on role and depth
- Basic authoring: 1-2 weeks
- Publishing/variants: 2-4 weeks
- Development/customization: 4-8 weeks
Use learning paths above to accelerate.
"What tools do I need?"
Answer: Publishing Guide → Tools
Required:
- DITA-OT (free, open-source)
- XML editor (Oxygen, VS Code, or text editor)
- Java 17+ (for DITA-OT)
Optional:
- CCMS (enterprise content management)
- Docker (for builds)
- Git (for version control)
"Can I migrate existing docs to DITA?"
Answer: Yes, common approaches:
- Manual conversion (highest quality, most effort)
- Scripted conversion (Markdown/HTML → DITA)
- Gradual migration (new content in DITA)
- Hybrid approach (convert high-value content first)
See Developer Handbook for migration strategies.
"How do I customize HTML output?"
Answer: XSLT Customization Tutorial
Two safe approaches:
- CSS only (styling, no structure changes)
- XSLT-import overrides (structure + styling)
Avoid modifying core DITA-OT files.

# Quick Start Guide
## 🚨 Troubleshooting First Steps
This quick start guide helps you navigate the documentation collection and begin working with DITA immediately.

Build fails?
- Check DITA-OT installation (dita --version)
- Verify Java version (java -version → 17+)
- Review error message for specific issue
- Check Architecture Diagram to identify stage
Content not appearing?
- Check DITAVAL filtering (conditional processing)
- Verify topic is referenced in map
- Check chunking settings
- Examine temp files in work directory
HTML looks wrong?
- Verify CSS path in build properties
- Check XSLT overrides are installed
- Clear browser cache
- Review generated HTML source
Need more help?
- Troubleshooting section
- Debugging playbook

# DITA at a Glance
## Introduction
DITA is designed for teams that need:

Darwin Information Typing Architecture (DITA) is an XML-based standard for writing topic-oriented, reusable technical content and publishing it to multiple outputs (HTML, PDF, help systems, portals, etc.). It’s maintained as an open standard by the OASIS DITA Technical Committee.

text:
# DITA at a Glance
## Why DITA exists (the problems it solves)
DITA is designed for teams that need:

DITA is designed for teams that need:
- Reuse at scale (the same warning/procedure used across products and manuals)
- Multiple deliverables from one source (HTML + PDF + embedded help)
- Product variants (editions, platforms, regions, customer-specific builds)
- Long-lived documentation with frequent updates
- Large teams needing structure, consistency, and governance
The core idea: write small, semantically meaningful modules (topics), assemble them with maps, and publish through a repeatable pipeline.

text:
# DITA at a Glance
## The DITA mental model
DITA is designed for teams that need:

Topics: small, typed units of information
DITA encourages authors to write content as topics: self-contained units that answer a user need. The most common base topic types are:
- Concept — explain “what/why”
- Task — explain “how”
- Reference — provide “facts/specs/options”
This style is called information typing: using different structures depending on the user intent.
Maps: assemble topics into deliverables
A DITA map is how you organize and publish:
- Defines the table of contents
- Controls navigation structure
- Can define relationships/links
- Defines keys (late-bound references)
- Often defines publishing context (for filtering/branding)
“Darwin”: specialization and evolution
DITA is built to evolve without breaking. You can extend (specialize) elements and constraints to fit your domain while keeping compatibility with tooling.

section_title: Key building blocks in the spec (what you’ll see in real projects)
text: # DITA at a Glance ## Key building blocks in the spec (what you’ll see in real projects) DITA is designed for teams that need: Topic anatomy (common elements) Most topics include: - : topic title - Body element depends on type: <conbody>, <taskbody>, <refbody> - Common inline structures: <p>, <ph>, <uicontrol>, <codeph>, etc. - Lists/tables: <ul>, <ol>, <simpletable>, <table> Task topics add specialized structure: - <prereq> (before you start) - <context> (why/when) - <steps> with <step><cmd>...</cmd></step> - <result> / <postreq> Reference topics commonly use: - <refbody> with sections or a reference table-like structure - <properties> (property/value definitions) in some models Maps: topicref and hierarchy The map primarily uses <topicref> to point to topics: ```xml <map> <title>Getting Started ``` DITA maps can also use: - : a heading without a linked topic - : grouping without appearing in TOC (tool-dependent behavior) - Relationship tables (advanced linking) Domains, constraints, and specialization (practical view) In real implementations you may see: - Domains: add sets of elements (e.g., UI domain, highlighting domain) - Constraints: restrict what’s allowed for a certain doc set - Specialization: define new elements as specializations of existing ones Rule of thumb: specialize only when you have a strong semantic or validation need and you can support it in publishing + authoring tools.

# DITA at a Glance ## The most important DITA features you’ll actually use DITA is designed for teams that need: Reuse with conref (content reference) conref pulls content from another location. Use cases - Shared warnings, notes, prerequisites - Shared UI labels - Shared steps or step fragments Example (reuse a note): ```xml

``` Best practice - Keep conref targets stable and well-named - Treat shared snippets as “mini APIs” (don’t casually restructure them) Keys + keyref (late-bound reuse and linking) Keys are defined in the map; topics reference them with keyref. Why keys matter: - Change a URL/product name once in the map → updates everywhere - Reuse topics in different publications with different key definitions - Avoid hard-coding product names and links in topics Example (in map): ```xml Acme Writer Pro ``` In a topic: ```xml

text:
# DITA at a Glance
## Publishing with DITA Open Toolkit (DITA-OT) (common approach)
DITA is designed for teams that need:

DITA-OT is the most widely used open-source engine for publishing DITA.
Basic commands (typical)
Generate HTML5:
```bash
dita -i guides/getting-started.ditamap -f html5 -o out/html
```
Generate PDF (toolchain dependent—usually FO-based):
```bash
dita -i guides/getting-started.ditamap -f pdf -o out/pdf
```
Repeatable builds with project files (recommended)
As doc sets grow, use project files to store:
- inputs
- outputs
- filters
- parameters
This makes builds consistent across machines/CI.
Where customization lives
Common customization layers include:
- Parameters (simple switches)
- CSS/HTML templates (HTML output)
- PDF plugins/themes (PDF output)
- Custom XSLT and DITA-OT plugins (advanced)
Practical advice: start with configuration + small overrides, then grow into plugins only when needed.

# DITA at a Glance
## Best practices (what prevents pain later)
DITA is designed for teams that need:

Write small topics with a single purpose
- Don’t write “chapters” as a single topic
- Prefer “one user goal = one topic”
Use keys for anything that changes across deliverables
- Product names, external URLs, reused references
- This keeps topics clean and portable
Be deliberate about conref
- Great for stable reusable fragments
- Overuse can make content hard to read and maintain
- Consider “reuse boundaries”: warnings, UI strings, legal text, standard steps
Standardize attribute vocabularies
- Define allowed values for platform/audience/product
- Enforce via review and/or constraints
Establish naming conventions
- Topic IDs and file names should be stable
- Use predictable IDs for conref targets
Validation and build hygiene
- Always validate during CI:
- well-formed XML   - schema/DTD validation (as applicable)   - broken links/conrefs/keys

text:
# DITA Deep Dive
## What DITA is (in practical terms)
If you've ever seen a documentation program struggle with:

Darwin Information Typing Architecture (DITA) is a standard for writing content as structured XML topics that can be reused, assembled, and published to multiple formats (HTML, PDF, help portals, etc.). The “architecture” part is important: DITA is not just a tag set. It is a way to design content so it can evolve and scale.
If you’ve ever seen a documentation program struggle with:
- repeated copy‑paste content,
- inconsistent terminology,
- many products/variants,
- many output formats,
- frequent releases and updates,
- multiple teams editing at once,
…DITA exists to reduce those problems by shifting from “write a big document” to “author modular components and assemble them.”

section_title: The DITA mindset: topics + maps + reuse + publishing
text:
# DITA Deep Dive
## The DITA mindset: topics + maps + reuse + publishing
If you've ever seen a documentation program struggle with:

Almost everything in DITA makes sense if you keep this mental picture:
- You author topics (small, reusable information units).
- You assemble topics into deliverables using maps (a map is the publishing “spine”).
- You reuse content using references (conref, keyref, conkeyref, etc.).
- You publish through a toolchain (often DITA‑OT, sometimes a CCMS with its own engine).
- You can evolve the model through specialization (the “Darwin” aspect).
DITA is designed for teams who want their content to behave like well‑engineered software:
- stable interfaces (IDs and reuse targets),
- modular design,
- controlled vocabulary,
- repeatable builds,
- automated validation.

text:
# DITA Deep Dive
## Why “Darwin” is in the name
If you've ever seen a documentation program struggle with:

“Darwin” signals adaptation. You can extend DITA to match your domain without abandoning the ecosystem. In practice, this means you can:
- specialize elements to represent domain concepts,
- create constraints that enforce standards,
- keep the base semantics recognizable so tools can still process it.
This is not “invent your own XML from scratch.” It’s “extend a known model safely.”

text:
# DITA Deep Dive
## Information typing: concept, task, reference
If you've ever seen a documentation program struggle with:

DITA popularized the idea that different user intents deserve different structures:
- Concept topics: explain what something is and why it matters.
- Task topics: explain how to do something step‑by‑step.
- Reference topics: list facts, fields, values, APIs, commands, parameters, tables.
Why this matters:
- It drives consistency (every task has prerequisites, steps, results).
- It helps readers find information faster (users know what to expect).
- It improves automation (transforms can format tasks differently than reference).
A DITA program that ignores information typing usually ends up with “generic topics” that are hard to maintain and publish consistently.

section_title: Core components you’ll see in real DITA programs
text:
# DITA Deep Dive
## Core components you’ll see in real DITA programs
If you've ever seen a documentation program struggle with:

Topics
Topics are files like:
- install.dita (task)
- what-is-dita.dita (concept)
- cli-options.dita (reference)
A topic typically includes:
- a stable id,
- a <title>,
- a body (taskbody/conbody/refbody),
- optional metadata in <prolog>.
Maps
Maps are files like:
- getting-started.ditamap
- admin-guide.ditamap
- api-reference.ditamap
A map:
- defines the publication structure (TOC),
- defines keys and sometimes scope,
- can define relationships,
- can define conditional processing rules indirectly (via build config).
Metadata
DITA supports structured metadata:
- authorship and versioning,
- product/platform/audience attributes,
- keywords and taxonomy,
- short descriptions and abstracts.
Metadata is not decoration. In an enterprise environment it drives:
- search relevance,
- portal faceting,
- translation segmentation,
- governance and review workflows.
Reuse mechanisms
DITA’s reuse is not just “copy content.” It includes:
- conref: reuse content by referencing a source element.
- keyref: refer to keys defined in a map (late binding).
- conkeyref: pull content via keys (flexible reuse across contexts).
- content references via maps (topic reuse by including the same topic in multiple maps).
Conditional processing (filtering)
DITA supports publishing different variants:
- Windows vs Linux,
- Basic vs Pro,
- Internal vs External,
- Region‑specific or customer‑specific.
This is typically done using:
- attributes in the source (e.g., platform="windows"),
- filtering rules in build config (e.g., DITAVAL in DITA‑OT).
Specialization and constraints
Advanced programs define:
- specialization to represent domain semantics,
- constraints to restrict the model and reduce variability.
Constraints are often more important than specialization because they control author behavior.

section_title: When DITA is a great fit
text:
# DITA Deep Dive
## When DITA is a great fit
If you've ever seen a documentation program struggle with:

DITA shines when you have at least one of these:
- Multiple deliverables from a single source (HTML + PDF + portal).
- High reuse (warnings, procedures, UI strings, legal statements).
- Complex products with many settings and options.
- Rapid releases with continuous updates.
- Multiple product variants requiring conditional content.
- Large author teams where structure reduces inconsistency.
If you have a single small doc set with minimal reuse and only HTML output, DITA can be more effort than it’s worth—unless your organization already runs a DITA platform and needs standardization.

text:
# DITA Deep Dive
## DITA vs. alternatives (practical comparison)
If you've ever seen a documentation program struggle with:

DITA vs Markdown
Markdown is lightweight and fast. DITA is structured and scalable.
Markdown is often ideal for:
- developer docs close to code,
- small teams,
- fast iteration with minimal publishing complexity.
DITA is often ideal for:
- complex product documentation,
- multi‑format deliverables,
- controlled reuse and variants,
- enterprise governance.
A hybrid strategy is common:
- engineers write in Markdown for dev-facing docs,
- tech pubs writes in DITA for user-facing manuals and regulated outputs,
- publishing pipelines unify the experience in a portal.
DITA vs DocBook
Both are XML-based publishing systems. DITA is topic-oriented and reuse-centric; DocBook is traditionally book/chapter oriented (though you can do modular structures). DITA’s key systems and specialization model are a differentiator for large reuse programs.
DITA vs S1000D
S1000D is a defense/aerospace standard with strict structures, data modules, and business rules. DITA is more flexible and widely used across industries. Organizations sometimes map between them, but they are not interchangeable.

text:
# DITA Deep Dive
## How DITA programs are organized (the “content system” view)
If you've ever seen a documentation program struggle with:

A successful DITA adoption is as much operational design as it is technology. Typical components:
- Content model
- which topic types you use,    - which domains are enabled,    - what constraints exist,    - how IDs and reuse targets are structured.
- Information architecture
- map patterns,    - navigation approach,    - link strategy,    - modularization and reuse boundaries.
- Toolchain
- authoring (XML editor, CCMS),    - validation and build automation,    - publishing engine and customization,    - review workflows.
- Governance
- naming conventions,    - controlled vocabularies for filtering,    - content ownership for shared snippets,    - change control for keys and reuse targets.
If you skip governance, DITA often becomes “XML chaos” instead of “structured content.”

text:
# DITA Deep Dive
## Common misconceptions (and the reality)
If you've ever seen a documentation program struggle with:

“DITA is just XML tags”
DITA is an ecosystem: modeling, reuse, build pipelines, and specialization.
“DITA automatically makes content reusable”
DITA provides mechanisms, but reuse requires design:
- stable IDs,
- consistent chunking,
- disciplined authoring,
- governance.
“You must use a CCMS to use DITA”
You can start with files + Git + DITA‑OT. A CCMS becomes valuable at scale for workflow, translation management, reuse governance, and metadata enforcement.
“Specialization is mandatory”
Most teams succeed using base DITA plus constraints and controlled vocabularies. Specialization is powerful but best introduced after you can publish and maintain reliably.

text:
# DITA Deep Dive
## What “good DITA” looks like
If you've ever seen a documentation program struggle with:

Good DITA content is:
- modular (each topic has a single purpose),
- semantically consistent (use task for procedures),
- reusable (common content is referenced, not copied),
- publishable (builds are repeatable and validated),
- governed (attributes and keys are controlled).
A quick self-check:
- Can you reuse the same topic in two different maps without editing it?
- Can you publish two product variants using filtering?
- Can you change a product name in one place (keys) and have it update everywhere?
- Can your build fail fast when references are broken?
If the answer is “yes,” you’re doing DITA the way it was designed.

# Filtering with DITAVAL and build variants (audience/platform)
## Introduction
DITA supports conditional processing using attributes such as audience and platform. DITA-OT applies filtering using a DITAVAL file referenced by args.filter.

Example DITAVAL files
Exclude print-only content for a WEB build:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<val>
  <prop action="exclude" att="platform" val="print"/>
</val>
Exclude novice-only content for an ADMIN build:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<val>
  <prop action="exclude" att="audience" val="novice"/>
</val>
Using filters in builds
dita -i map.ditamap -f html5 -o out/html5-web   --args.filter=build/ditaval/web.ditaval
dita -i map.ditamap -f html5 -o out/html5-print --args.filter=build/ditaval/print.ditaval

dita -i map.ditamap -f html5 -o out/html5-admin  --args.filter=build/ditaval/audience-admin.ditaval
dita -i map.ditamap -f html5 -o out/html5-novice --args.filter=build/ditaval/audience-novice.ditaval
Best practice: properties files per variant
Store stable settings in properties files:
build/html5-sidebar.properties (base)
  build/html5-sidebar-web.properties (base + args.filter=...)
  build/html5-sidebar-print.properties (base + args.filter=...)
  build/html5-sidebar-admin.properties (base + args.filter=...)
  build/html5-sidebar-novice.properties (base + args.filter=...)
This prevents "mystery builds" and makes CI reproducible.
Common variant patterns you'll use in real projects
Platform split: web vs print output styles, image sizes, navigation density
  Audience split: admin vs end-user content, warnings, extra setup steps
  Feature tiers: community vs enterprise (often driven by product or custom props)
  Region/language: often via map roots + language settings; DITAVAL can help when content is shared

text:
# Write your first override XSLT correctly ## Introduction Your override stylesheet imports the default HTML5 transform and overrides only the templates you need, using xsl:next-match for safe layering. Import the default HTML5 implementation Your override stylesheet should start by importing the default HTML5 transform: Precedence rules (do not skip) templates in your override file win over imported templates within the same precedence level, more specific matches win import order matters if you have multiple imports Practical implication: keep overrides in a single file (or layered imports you control) and avoid rewriting entire default templates unless necessary. Prefer xsl:next-match for safe layering When overriding a template but wanting default behavior, wrap with xsl:next-match:
This is safer than copy-pasting large default templates.

text:
# What you're customizing (HTML5 transform architecture)
## Introduction
DITA-OT publishing is a pipeline. Understanding its three phases — preprocessing, XSLT transformation, and post-processing — is essential before writing any override.

In DITA-OT 4.x, HTML5 output involves:
Preprocessing
Typical preprocessing steps include:
map parsing (reading the map + topicrefs)
  key collection (keydefs → key space)
  conref/keyref resolution (pulling referenced content)
  filtering / conditional processing (DITAVAL / props / audience/platform/product)
  chunking decisions (map-driven chunk strategy)
  copying/transient file preparation in the temp folder
Why this matters: Many "mysterious" HTML issues are not XSLT bugs; they come from preprocessing (e.g., conref not resolved, chunking not applied, keys not in scope).
Transformation to HTML (XSLT)
After preprocessing, the HTML5 pipeline runs XSLT to generate:
topic pages (chunked or not)
  navigation artifacts such as TOC pages
  HTML element mappings for DITA structures (p, section, note, steps, tables, xref, etc.)
Your XSLT customizations live here. This is where you:
modify HTML structure
  add CSS classes and data-* attributes
  change output for specific DITA elements
  inject JS/CSS references
  create wrappers for responsive layouts
Post-processing
After HTML is generated, DITA-OT typically:
copies CSS and other assets
  writes support files (images, JS if configured/added)
  finalizes output folder structure
Why this matters: Your output may look wrong simply because your CSS/JS wasn't copied into output.
Extension points: the supported integration points
DITA-OT exposes official extension points that let you plug into the pipeline without editing core files.
The most common for HTML5:
dita.xsl.html5 — override or extend the main HTML5 XSLT step
  dita.xsl.html5.toc — override or extend TOC generation XSLT
  dita.conductor.html5.param and dita.conductor.html5.toc.param — pass parameters into the main HTML5 and TOC steps
  general plug-in XSLT import extension points (import-based override mechanism)
The key idea: create a plug-in that imports the default HTML5 XSLT and overrides only the templates you need.

topic_title: Hardening for production (CI, quality gates, upgrade plan)
text:
# Hardening for production (CI, quality gates, upgrade plan)
## Introduction
Pin your toolkit version, set up CI pipelines for all build variants, and follow a deliberate upgrade strategy when moving to new DITA-OT releases.

Version pinning
Pin a toolkit version per release line and upgrade deliberately.
CI pipeline (minimum)
install plug-ins (dita --install)
  run builds (all variants)
  run in strict mode where appropriate
  publish build artifacts for review
Upgrade strategy
When upgrading DITA-OT:
run builds with no customization changes
  compare outputs
  adjust imports (TOC imports are common)
  minimize template rewrites
Maintain a CHANGELOG.md in your plug-in repo.

# Production patterns for all topic types + common elements
## Scaling approach
A core set of XSLT override templates that scale across all DITA topic types and common elements using @class-based matching and xsl:next-match wrappers.

To scale to "all topic types + common elements" without creating a fragile customization layer:
- match topic roots using topic/topic ancestry
- match common elements using @class ancestry
- wrap default behavior with xsl:next-match wherever possible
- apply consistent classes and data-* attributes for styling and instrumentation

# Production patterns for all topic types + common elements
## Standardize the topic wrapper (applies to all topic types)
A core set of XSLT override templates that scale across all DITA topic types and common elements using @class-based matching and xsl:next-match wrappers.

```xslt
<xsl:template match="*[contains(@class,' topic/topic ')]">
  <article class="dita-topic" data-topic-type="{local-name()}">
    <xsl:apply-templates select="*[contains(@class,' topic/title ')]"/>
    <xsl:apply-templates select="*[contains(@class,' topic/shortdesc ')]"/>

    <main class="dita-body">
      <xsl:apply-templates
        select="*[contains(@class,' topic/body ')]
              | *[contains(@class,' concept/conbody ')]
              | *[contains(@class,' task/taskbody ')]
              | *[contains(@class,' reference/refbody ')]"/>
    </main>

    <xsl:apply-templates select="*[contains(@class,' topic/related-links ')]"/>
  </article>
</xsl:template>
```

- Avoid copying large default templates unless absolutely necessary
- Comment intent and expected output per override

topic_title: Two safe strategies for HTML5 customization
text:
# Two safe strategies for HTML5 customization
## Introduction
There are two reliable approaches to customizing DITA-OT HTML5 output: a plug-in with XSLT import overrides (recommended) or build-time custom XSL via args.xsl.

Strategy A (recommended): DITA-OT plug-in + XSLT import overrides
You create a plug-in that:
depends on the HTML5 plug-in (org.dita.html5)
  provides an override XSL file via dita.xsl.html5 and/or dita.xsl.html5.toc
  imports the default transform and overrides specific templates
Why it's best long-term:
clean separation (your code is isolated)
  controlled load order (dependencies)
  easier upgrades (you don't edit toolkit core)
Strategy B: Build-time custom XSL via args.xsl
You supply a single XSL file at build time (parameter-driven). Good for:
experiments
  temporary branches
  customer-specific builds that should not live in your core plugin
Risk: unmanaged sprawl (multiple XSLs floating around, unclear precedence, harder upgrades).
Rule of thumb:
customization is part of your product pipeline → plug-in
  one-off/experimental → args.xsl

topic_title: DITA-OT 4.x setup: sidebar TOC + chunking baseline
text:
# DITA-OT 4.x setup: sidebar TOC + chunking baseline
## Introduction
Before writing XSLT, establish a baseline build that gives you chunked pages, a sidebar TOC on every page, and repeatable builds via properties files.

Sidebar TOC embedded in every page: nav-toc=full
In DITA-OT HTML5 builds, nav-toc=full embeds a TOC <nav> in each topic page:
dita -i path/to/map.ditamap -f html5 -o out/html5 --nav-toc=full
Repeatable builds: use a .properties file
Create build/html5-sidebar.properties:
# Sidebar TOC embedded in every topic page
nav-toc=full

# Generate an index TOC file
html5.toc.generate=yes

# Include DITA @class in output to aid debugging and CSS hooks
args.html5.classattr=yes

# Brand CSS
args.copycss=yes
args.cssroot=css/
args.css=brand.css
Build with:
dita -i path/to/map.ditamap -f html5 -o out/html5 --propertyfile=build/html5-sidebar.properties
Chunking: map-driven, scalable
Chunking is usually controlled in the map using @chunk. A common pattern:
<topicref href="topics/intro.dita" chunk="to-content">
  <topicref href="topics/install.dita"/>
  <topicref href="topics/configure.dita"/>
</topicref>
That yields separate HTML pages per child topic, with a TOC that links to those pages.

text:
# TOC grouping with topichead/topicgroup (non-clickable headings)
## Introduction
Use topichead and topicgroup elements to create non-clickable navigation headings that organize the sidebar TOC without requiring landing pages.

For clean navigation headings that are non-clickable, use:
<topichead navtitle="..."> ... </topichead> for headings
  <topicgroup navtitle="..."> ... </topicgroup> for grouping containers
Example:
<map>
  <title>Docs</title>

  <topichead navtitle="Getting Started">
    <topicref href="topics/intro.dita"/>
    <topicref href="topics/install.dita"/>
  </topichead>

  <topicgroup navtitle="Reference">
    <topicref href="topics/cli.dita"/>
    <topicref href="topics/api.dita"/>
  </topicgroup>
</map>
This keeps the sidebar TOC tidy without forcing landing pages for every group.
text_plain:
For clean navigation headings that are non-clickable, use:
<topichead navtitle="..."> ... </topichead> for headings
  <topicgroup navtitle="..."> ... </topicgroup> for grouping containers
Example:

topic_title: Prerequisites: what you need to know before overriding
text:
# Prerequisites: what you need to know before overriding
## Introduction
Before writing XSLT overrides you need fluency in template matching, modes, import precedence, and how DITA's @class attribute drives element processing.

XSLT basics you must understand
You should be comfortable with:
template matching (match=)
  modes (mode=)
  named templates (name=)
  xsl:import vs xsl:include (import precedence matters)
  how to build/copy attributes and preserve content
  how to layer changes safely:
xsl:next-match (often the best)
  xsl:apply-imports (useful in some stylesheets)
  overriding named templates (when the default is structured that way)
DITA basics that affect HTML transforms
DITA content is specialized. The @class attribute encodes ancestry such as:
topic/p (paragraph)
  topic/note (note)
  task/step (task step)
DITA-OT templates often match using ancestry patterns:
match="*[contains(@class,' topic/p ')]"
Why it matters: matching by @class supports both base elements and specializations that inherit those semantics.

text:
# Troubleshooting
## XSL overrides do not show up
Common issues and solutions for sidebar TOC, chunking, XSL overrides, external links, and post-upgrade breakages.

- confirm your plug-in is installed in the correct DITA-OT instance
- run dita --install
- verify plugin.xml has require plugin="org.dita.html5"
- verify extension points are correct (dita.xsl.html5, dita.xsl.html5.toc)

# DITA-OT Architecture Diagram
## DITA-OT HTML5 pipeline overview
ASCII reference diagram showing the complete DITA-OT HTML5 processing pipeline from inputs through preprocessing, transformation, and output.

[Figure: DITA-OT (HTML5) Architecture — preprocess vs preprocess2]
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         DITA-OT (HTML5) ARCHITECTURE — with preprocess2 option               │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

INPUTS (your repo)
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ • DITA map (.ditamap) + topics (.dita)                                                        │
│ • Resources (images/media/code)                                                               │
│ • DITAVAL filters (audience/platform/product/props)                                           │
│ • Plug-ins (XSLT / Ant / Java) + build properties (.properties / CLI params)                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                         |
                                         v
SELECT PREPROCESSING PIPELINE (HTML5)
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Choose ONE (depends on toolkit/version/transtype configuration):                               │
│                                                                                               │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │ A) preprocess  (legacy/mixed) │    OR      │ B) preprocess2 (map-first)               │   │
│   │ map + topic steps interleave  │            │ complete map processing first, then      │   │
│   └───────────────┬───────────────┘            │ process topics after map features are     │   │
│                   │                            │ resolved                                  │   │
│                   │                            └───────────────────┬──────────────────────┘   │
└───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┘
                    |                                                |
                    v                                                v
COMMON PREPROCESSING RESPONSIBILITIES  (both pipelines produce the same "normalized" intent)
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 1) Map parsing + key collection                                                               │
│    • keydefs, key scopes, key space                                                           │
│ 2) Conditional processing / filtering                                                         │
│    • DITAVAL/props applied → content included/excluded                                        │
│ 3) Reference resolution                                                                       │
│    • conref / conkeyref / keyref / coderef                                                    │
│ 4) Pull processing                                                                            │
│    • metadata pulls, navtitle/title behavior                                                  │
│ 5) Chunking decisions + job file lists                                                        │
│    • "what becomes which HTML file/page"                                                      │
│ 6) Relationship/link metadata                                                                 │
│    • maplink, rel tables                                                                      │
│                                                                                               │
│ RESULT: normalized + resolved intermediate content                                             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                         |
                                         v
TEMP WORKSPACE (work dir / temp dir)
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ • normalized / filtered / resolved topics + maps                                              │
│ • job configuration + file lists (what to build)                                              │
│ • logs + diagnostics                                                                          │
│                                                                                               │
│ Debug rule: if intermediates are wrong, HTML5 output will be wrong.                           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                         |
                                         v
HTML5 PROCESSING (format-specific)
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ A) HTML5 XSLT transform                                                                       │
│    • converts normalized DITA to HTML markup                                                  │
│    • applies your XSLT import overrides (if plug-in installed)                                │
│                                                                                               │
│ B) Navigation / TOC generation                                                                │
│    • index/TOC artifacts                                                                      │
│    • optional embedded nav per page (e.g., nav-toc=full)                                      │
│                                                                                               │
│ C) Post-processing / packaging                                                                │
│    • copy CSS/JS/images                                                                       │
│    • add extra assets (search index, analytics hooks, etc.)                                   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                         |

text:
# Glossary
Comprehensive DITA and DITA-OT terminology used throughout the Extense training documentation. Each term includes definitions, abbreviations, usage guidance, and scope notes for chatbot knowledge retrieval.

Comprehensive DITA and DITA-OT terminology used throughout the Extense training documentation. Each term includes definitions, abbreviations, usage guidance, and scope notes for chatbot knowledge retrieval.
Extense LLC
    
    Glossary
        Terminology
        DITA terms
        Knowledge base
      taxonomy:reference
      all
Topic
    A modular, self-contained unit of content in DITA. Topics are the fundamental building blocks and come in typed flavors (concept, task, reference) to match reader intent.
    DITA topic
      Use "topic" when referring to any standalone DITA content unit. Specify the type (concept topic, task topic) when the distinction matters.
      Applicable to all DITA versions (1.0+). Topics are the core unit of structured authoring.
Concept
    A DITA topic type for explanatory, background, or "what is" content. Concepts answer questions like "What is X?" and "Why does X matter?" They use the <concept> root element and <conbody> for content.
    concept topic
      Write concepts for introductory, explanatory, or background material. Do not include step-by-step procedures in a concept — use a task topic instead.
      One of the three core DITA information types.
Task
    A DITA topic type for procedural, step-by-step content. Tasks answer "How do I do X?" and use structured <steps> elements with <cmd> and optional <info>, <stepresult>, and <stepxmp> sub-elements.
    task topic
      Use task topics for any procedural content. Include <prereq> for prerequisites, <context> for background, and <result> for expected outcomes.
      One of the three core DITA information types. DITA also supports "general task" for less-structured procedures.
Reference
    A DITA topic type for lookup-oriented content such as API references, parameter tables, CLI options, or specifications. Uses the <reference> root element with <refbody> containing tables, property lists, or syntax diagrams.
    reference topic
      Use reference topics for content that users look up rather than read sequentially. Structure with tables and definition lists for scannability.
      One of the three core DITA information types.
Map
    An assembly structure that organizes topics into a hierarchy for publishing. Maps define the table of contents, navigation, and relationship tables. A map does not contain content directly — it references topics.
    DITA map
      ditamap
      Use maps to assemble topics into deliverables. A single topic can appear in multiple maps. Use bookmaps for book-like outputs with front/back matter.
      Maps use the .ditamap file extension. Bookmaps are a specialization of maps.
Bookmap
    A specialization of DITA map that adds book-oriented structures like parts, chapters, appendices, front matter, and back matter. Used for producing book-like deliverables (PDF, printed manuals).
    DITA bookmap
      Use bookmaps when your output needs book structure (chapters, parts, appendices, TOC, index). Use plain maps for simpler HTML or help outputs.
      Available in DITA 1.1+. Requires the bookmap DTD/schema.
Information Typing
    The practice of using different topic structures (concept, task, reference) to match different reader intents and optimize the user experience. This is a core DITA design principle.
    information typing
      Apply information typing by asking: "Is this content explaining something (concept), showing how to do something (task), or providing lookup data (reference)?"
      Foundational to the DITA architecture. Custom specializations can add domain-specific types.
Reuse
    Referencing content instead of duplicating it. DITA provides three primary reuse mechanisms: conref (content reference), keyref (key reference), and conkeyref (key-based content reference). Reuse reduces translation cost, editing effort, and inconsistency.
    content reuse
      Design content for reuse by keeping topics self-contained, using conref for shared blocks, keyref for variable text, and DITAVAL for variant filtering.
      Reuse is often cited as the primary economic justification for adopting DITA.
Conref
    Content reference — an attribute that pulls content from another element by matching its @id. The referenced content replaces the referencing element at processing time. This is "copy by reference" at the element level.
    content reference (conref)
      conref
      Use conref to share identical content (warnings, notes, boilerplate paragraphs) across topics. The source and target elements must be the same type.
      Available in DITA 1.0+. For key-based content pulling, see conkeyref.
Keyref
    Key reference — resolves links and variable text via keys defined in the DITA map. Keys provide late binding: the same key can resolve differently in different maps or key scopes, enabling context-dependent content.
    key reference (keyref)
      keyref
      Use keyrefs for product names, version numbers, and links that may change between deliverables. Define keys in maps using <keydef>.
      Available in DITA 1.2+. Key scopes (DITA 1.3) enable multiple definitions of the same key in different contexts.
Conkeyref
    Combines keys and content pulling — a key-based content reference that resolves the source element via a key definition, then pulls the content like a conref. Enables flexible multi-context reuse.
    key-based content reference (conkeyref)
      conkeyref
      Use conkeyref when the reuse source varies by context (e.g., different product editions pull from different warehouse topics).
      Available in DITA 1.2+. Requires key definitions in the map.
Filtering
    Producing content variants by including or excluding elements based on conditional attributes (audience, platform, product, otherprops) and DITAVAL rules. Also called "conditional processing" or "profiling."
    conditional filtering
      DITAVAL filtering
      Apply filtering attributes to elements, then use DITAVAL files to include/exclude/flag content at build time. Plan your filtering strategy before authoring.
      DITA provides four built-in filtering attributes: audience, platform, product, and otherprops.
DITAVAL

text:
# Glossary
Comprehensive DITA and DITA-OT terminology used throughout the Extense training documentation. Each term includes definitions, abbreviations, usage guidance, and scope notes for chatbot knowledge retrieval.

    An XML file that defines conditional processing rules (include, exclude, flag) applied during DITA-OT builds. DITAVAL files control which content variants appear in the output.
    DITAVAL file
      Create separate DITAVAL files for each product variant or audience. Reference them in build commands with --filter= or in DITA-OT project files.
      DITAVAL is processed during the filtering stage of DITA-OT preprocessing.
Specialization
    Extending DITA's type system to represent domain-specific concepts while maintaining compatibility with standard DITA processing. Specializations inherit from base types and can add constraints or new elements.
    DITA specialization
      Consider specialization when your domain has recurring structures that don't fit standard topic types. Always specialize from the closest existing base type.
      The "Darwin" in DITA refers to this evolutionary inheritance model.
Constraints
    Restricting the DITA content model to enforce consistency — for example, removing optional elements or requiring certain metadata. Constraints tighten the model without adding new elements.
    DITA constraints
      Use constraints to enforce organization-specific authoring rules (e.g., require shortdesc, prohibit nested topics).
      Available in DITA 1.2+. Constraints are complementary to specialization.
Key Scope
    A named context within a DITA map that limits key definitions to a specific branch. Key scopes enable the same key name to resolve differently in different parts of the same map.
    key scope
      keyscope
      Use key scopes when a single deliverable needs different values for the same key. Define with @keyscope on topicref elements.
      Available in DITA 1.3+.
Branch Filtering
    Applying DITAVAL filtering rules to a specific branch (subtree) of a DITA map rather than the entire map. This enables multiple filtered variants within a single build.
    branch filtering
      Use <ditavalref> elements on map branches to apply variant-specific filtering without separate builds.
      Available in DITA 1.3+. Processed during the DITA-OT branch-filter preprocessing step.
Relationship Table
    A table structure in DITA maps that defines non-hierarchical relationships between topics. Each row links related concepts, tasks, and references, generating "Related links" sections in the output.
    relationship table (reltable)
      reltable
      Use relationship tables instead of inline cross-references when link relationships should be managed centrally in the map.
      Relationship tables are processed during DITA-OT preprocessing to generate related-links.
Subject Scheme
    A special DITA map type that defines controlled vocabularies and taxonomies for metadata values. Subject schemes constrain attribute values to a predefined list, enabling consistent classification.
    subject scheme map
      Create subject scheme maps to define enumerated values for filtering attributes. This prevents inconsistent metadata and enables AI-readable taxonomies.
      Available in DITA 1.2+. Uses the subjectScheme DTD/schema.
DITA Open Toolkit
    An open-source publishing engine that transforms DITA XML content into deliverable formats (HTML5, PDF, EPUB, etc.). DITA-OT provides a multi-stage processing pipeline with extension points for customization.
    DITA Open Toolkit (DITA-OT)
      DITA-OT
      DITA-OT is the standard publishing tool for DITA content. Most CCMS products use DITA-OT internally. Current version: 4.3.
      Open-source project at github.com/dita-ot/dita-ot. Written in Java with XSLT transforms.
Transtype
    The output format identifier used by DITA-OT to select a transformation (e.g., html5, pdf, markdown). Each transtype maps to a set of processing modules and XSLT stylesheets.
    transformation type (transtype)
      Specify the transtype with -f or --format in DITA-OT commands. Custom plugins can register new transtypes.
      Built-in transtypes include html5, pdf, xhtml, eclipsehelp, and markdown.
Preprocessing
    The first stage of DITA-OT processing that resolves references, applies filtering, normalizes content, and produces intermediate files. DITA-OT offers two pipelines: legacy "preprocess" and map-first "preprocess2."
    DITA-OT preprocessing (preprocess / preprocess2)
      preprocess2
      Use preprocess2 (map-first) for HTML5 output — it is faster and produces cleaner intermediate files.
      preprocess2 is available in DITA-OT 3.0+ and is the default for HTML5 in DITA-OT 4.x.
Plugin
    An extension package for DITA-OT that can inject XSLT overrides, Ant targets, custom transtypes, processing modules, and static resources. Plugins are the standard way to customize DITA-OT output.
    DITA-OT plugin (plug-in)
      plugin
      Create plugins for any output customization. Use the plugin.xml descriptor to declare dependencies and extension point contributions.
      Plugin IDs follow reverse-domain naming (e.g., com.extense.html5.branded).
Extension Point
    A named hook in the DITA-OT processing pipeline where plugins can contribute behavior — XSLT overrides, Ant targets, parameters, or resources.
    DITA-OT extension point
      Key extension points: dita.xsl.html5 (HTML5 XSLT), dita.xsl.html5.toc (TOC), dita.conductor.target (Ant targets).
      Extension points are documented in the DITA-OT developer reference.
Intermediate Files
    Normalized, filtered XML content produced during preprocessing and used by later processing stages. Found in the DITA-OT temp directory.
    DITA-OT intermediate files
      Inspect intermediate files in the temp/ directory to debug preprocessing issues.
      Intermediate files include resolved conrefs, applied filtering, and normalized metadata.
Chunking
    How DITA-OT decides to split or combine topics into output pages or files. Controlled by the @chunk attribute on map elements.
    DITA chunking
      Set @chunk="to-content" to combine child topics into one file. Use @chunk="by-topic" to split nested topics.
      Chunking behavior changed significantly in DITA 1.3 and DITA-OT 4.x.
Outputclass
text_plain:
An XML file that defines conditional processing rules (include, exclude, flag) applied during DITA-OT builds. DITAVAL files control which content variants appear in the output.
    DITAVAL file
      Create separate DITAVAL files for each product variant or audience. Reference them in build commands with --filter= or in DITA-OT project files.
      DITAVAL is processed during the filtering stage of DITA-OT preprocessing.
Specialization
    Extending DITA's type system to represent domain-specific concepts while maintaining compatibility with standard DITA processing. Specializations inherit from base types and can add constraints or new elements.
    DITA specialization
      Consider specialization when your domain has recurring structures that don't fit standard topic types. Always specialize from the closest existing base type.
      The "Darwin" in DITA refers to this evolutionary inheritance model.
Constraints
    Restricting the DITA content model to enforce consistency — for example, removing optional elements or requiring certain metadata. Constraints tighten the model without adding new elements.
    DITA constraints
      Use constraints to enforce organization-specific authoring rules (e.g., require shortdesc, prohibit nested topics).
      Available in DITA 1.2+. Constraints are complementary to specialization.
Key Scope
    A named context within a DITA map that limits key definitions to a specific branch. Key scopes enable the same key name to resolve differently in different parts of the same map.
    key scope
      keyscope
      Use key scopes when a single deliverable needs different values for the same key. Define with @keyscope on topicref elements.
      Available in DITA 1.3+.
Branch Filtering
    Applying DITAVAL filtering rules to a specific branch (subtree) of a DITA map rather than the entire map. This enables multiple filtered variants within a single build.
    branch filtering
      Use <ditavalref> elements on map branches to apply variant-specific filtering without separate builds.
      Available in DITA 1.3+. Processed during the DITA-OT branch-filter preprocessing step.

    A special DITA map type that defines controlled vocabularies and taxonomies for metadata values. Subject schemes constrain attribute values to a predefined list, enabling consistent classification.

# Glossary
Comprehensive DITA and DITA-OT terminology used throughout the Extense training documentation. Each term includes definitions, abbreviations, usage guidance, and scope notes for chatbot knowledge retrieval.

    A DITA attribute that passes styling hints to the output. The value is typically rendered as a CSS class name in HTML output or a formatting attribute in PDF.
    outputclass attribute
      Use outputclass for visual customization that doesn't change content meaning. Example: <codeblock outputclass="language-xml">
      Available on all DITA elements. Processed by XSLT transforms during output generation.
Project File
    A DITA-OT project file (.xml) that defines multiple deliverables, contexts, and publications in a single build configuration.
    DITA-OT project file
      Define deliverables in a project file to build multiple outputs from a single command: dita --project=myproject.xml
      Available in DITA-OT 3.4+.
Component Content Management System
    A content management system designed for component-level (topic-level) content. A CCMS manages DITA topics, maps, images, and metadata as individual components with version control, workflow, and publishing integration.
    Component Content Management System (CCMS)
      CCMS
      Consider a CCMS when your team needs component-level version control, workflow management, and multi-author collaboration beyond what file-based systems like Git provide.
      Extense provides CCMS implementation and integration services.
Structured Content
    Content authored with explicit semantic markup (XML, DITA, etc.) that separates content from presentation, enables reuse, and supports automated processing.
    structured content
      Structured content is the foundation of scalable documentation programs. DITA is the most widely adopted structured content standard for technical documentation.
      Extense specializes in structured content strategy and migration from unstructured to structured formats.
Content Migration
    The process of converting existing documentation from unstructured formats (Word, FrameMaker, HTML, Markdown) into structured DITA XML. Includes analysis, information architecture design, conversion, and validation.
    content migration
      Plan content migration by first analyzing existing content, designing the DITA information architecture, then converting with a combination of automated tools and manual review.
      Extense offers end-to-end content migration services.
Intelligent Content
    Content that is structurally rich, semantically categorized, and machine-processable — enabling automated discovery, personalization, and AI-driven delivery.
    intelligent content
      Transform standard DITA content into intelligent content by adding rich metadata, controlled vocabularies, content graphs, and machine-readable annotations.
      This training guide demonstrates intelligent content principles through its subject scheme, enriched prologs, and chatbot-ready metadata.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation
    An AI architecture that enhances language model responses by retrieving relevant content from a knowledge base before generating answers. RAG combines vector search over document chunks with LLM generation for grounded, accurate responses.
    Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
      RAG
      Structure DITA content for RAG by ensuring each topic has clear metadata, a good shortdesc, and proper chunking boundaries at section level.
      The Extense chatbot uses RAG over DITA-derived content chunks.

text:
# Authoring DITA Topics
## What “authoring in DITA” really means
Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It's writing with:

Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It’s writing with:
- a content model (what elements are allowed where),
- a purpose (concept vs task vs reference),
- a reuse strategy (what stays local vs shared),
- a publishing strategy (HTML/PDF/portal),
- a governance strategy (consistent attributes, IDs, terminology).
When DITA authoring goes wrong, it’s usually not because XML is hard. It’s because the team never agreed on:
- topic size,
- naming conventions,
- metadata patterns,
- reuse boundaries,
- filtering rules.
This topic gives you a practical, “how real teams do it” guide.

section_title: Topic design: picking the right topic type
text: # Authoring DITA Topics ## Topic design: picking the right topic type Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It's writing with: Concept topics (what/why) Use concept topics to explain: - definitions and mental models, - background and rationale, - architectural overview, - decisions and tradeoffs, - “when to use” and “when not to use.” A good concept topic: - answers a single question, - avoids procedural steps, - uses examples sparingly and purposefully. Common elements: - - <shortdesc> (optional but often valuable) - <conbody> with <p>, lists, sections as needed Task topics (how) Use tasks to instruct: - installation steps, - configuration procedures, - workflows and checklists, - troubleshooting actions (when they are step-based). A good task topic: - begins with prerequisites and context, - has clear, testable steps, - distinguishes commands/UI actions, - ends with results or next steps. Common elements: - <taskbody> - <prereq>, <context>, <steps>, <result>, <postreq> Reference topics (facts/options) Use reference topics for: - command syntax, - configuration parameters, - API endpoint specs, - field definitions, - error code tables. A good reference topic: - is scannable, - uses tables consistently, - avoids long narrative explanations, - includes examples where needed.

section_title: The most important authoring decision: topic scope (“chunking”)
text:
# Authoring DITA Topics
## The most important authoring decision: topic scope (“chunking”)
Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It's writing with:

Why chunking matters
Chunking is how you decide what is:
- one topic,
- a section inside a topic,
- a set of topics assembled by a map.
Chunking affects:
- reuse,
- translation cost,
- search relevance,
- link stability,
- maintenance.
Practical chunking guidelines
Make a topic when:
- the content solves one user goal,
- the content can be reused elsewhere,
- the content could appear in search results on its own.
Make a section (not a new topic) when:
- the content is only meaningful in the same context,
- splitting would create “orphan topics” with unclear purpose.
Split a topic if:
- it has multiple distinct goals,
- it becomes too long to scan,
- it mixes concept and procedure heavily,
- it gets reused partially (a sign you need smaller building blocks).
A simple heuristic
If a topic title needs “and” or “then” often, consider splitting:
- “Install and Configure the System” → likely two tasks or a concept + task.
- “What It Is and How to Use It” → concept + task.

section_title: IDs, filenames, and stability (treat your content like an API)
text:
# Authoring DITA Topics
## IDs, filenames, and stability (treat your content like an API)
Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It's writing with:

Why IDs matter more than you think
DITA references rely on IDs:
- conref targets,
- xrefs,
- map references,
- CCMS reuse references.
If IDs change frequently, you break:
- reuse,
- links,
- downstream systems and portals.
Recommended patterns
File names
- Use lowercase with hyphens: install-windows.dita
- Keep names stable over time
- Avoid “v2” or dates unless truly necessary
Topic IDs
- Match the filename or a stable semantic label:
- file: install.dita   - topic id: install
- Avoid auto-generated IDs like topic_1234 if you can
Element IDs for reuse targets
- Use meaningful IDs for reusable blocks:
- install-note   - admin-warning   - prereq-java
Stability rule
- Treat changing an ID like changing a public API:
- do it rarely,   - communicate it,   - update dependent references,   - validate builds.

section_title: Writing style in DITA (structure first, prose second)
text:
# Authoring DITA Topics
## Writing style in DITA (structure first, prose second)
Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It's writing with:

DITA is not a prose-first system. It’s structure-first:
- the structure communicates intent to both humans and machines,
- the prose fills in the details.
Task writing best practices
- Use imperative verbs: “Click”, “Select”, “Run”, “Verify”.
- One action per step.
- Put expected results in <result> or a final step.
- Separate UI and command line procedures if they diverge significantly.
- Use <uicontrol>, <menucascade>, <filepath>, <cmdname> when available (domain-dependent).
Concept writing best practices
- Start with a crisp definition or summary.
- Use short paragraphs.
- Use examples and diagrams deliberately.
- Avoid hidden tasks (“Do X, do Y”) unless it’s truly conceptual.
Reference writing best practices
- Use consistent tables for parameters/options.
- Use stable ordering (alphabetical or logical grouping).
- Provide defaults, constraints, and examples.

text:
# Authoring DITA Topics
## Authoring for reuse: designing “reusable chunks”
Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It's writing with:

Reuse is not free. It requires deliberate boundaries.
What should be reusable?
- warnings, cautions, safety notes,
- prerequisites and environment statements,
- legal/compliance text,
- UI labels and product naming,
- standard procedures used across many tasks,
- shared troubleshooting steps,
- consistent definitions.
What should usually NOT be reused?
- highly contextual narrative,
- content that changes often,
- content that’s meaningful only in one location,
- deep cross-referenced paragraphs that assume surrounding text.
“Reusable chunk” rules
A reusable chunk should:
- make sense when read alone,
- avoid “above/below” references,
- avoid map-specific context,
- be stable over time.
If it depends on context, keep it local.

text:
# Authoring DITA Topics
## Images, UI screenshots, and media management
Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It's writing with:

Image authoring best practices
- Keep images in a dedicated folder: media/images/.
- Use consistent naming: ui-settings-panel.png.
- Use alt text and/or text equivalents where required.
- Prefer vector (SVG) where your pipeline supports it and where clarity matters.
Screenshot governance
Screenshots are expensive to maintain. DITA doesn’t solve that automatically. To reduce screenshot maintenance:
- minimize screenshot dependence,
- use callouts sparingly,
- document UI actions in text with <uicontrol> elements,
- standardize screenshot production workflow.

text:
# Authoring DITA Topics
## Translation and localization implications
Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It's writing with:

DITA’s structure can reduce translation cost, but only if you:
- reuse content instead of duplicating it,
- keep topics stable,
- avoid rewriting shared snippets frequently,
- keep IDs stable (for reuse references).
Practical localization tips
- Don’t embed locale-specific text in shared snippets unless that’s intentional.
- Use keys for product names and terms that differ by region.
- Avoid hard-coded date formats and culturally specific examples in globally reused topics.

section_title: Common authoring mistakes (and how to fix them)
text:
# Authoring DITA Topics
## Common authoring mistakes (and how to fix them)
Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It's writing with:

Mistake 1: “Generic topics everywhere”
Fix: adopt information typing (concept/task/reference) and enforce it via review.
Mistake 2: “Giant topics”
Fix: split into smaller topics and assemble with maps.
Mistake 3: “Conref everywhere”
Fix: reuse only stable, context-independent chunks; document ownership and change control.
Mistake 4: “Uncontrolled attributes”
Fix: define a controlled vocabulary and enforce it (subject scheme + process).
Mistake 5: “IDs keep changing”
Fix: treat IDs as stable contracts; manage renames with care.

text:
# Authoring DITA Topics
## A practical authoring workflow for teams
Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It's writing with:

Here’s a workflow that works for both file-based and CCMS environments:
- Plan: create map skeleton (TOC) and topic list.
- Draft: create topic stubs with titles and shortdesc.
- Fill: author content following templates.
- Review: SME + editorial; check structure and semantics.
- Reuse pass: consolidate repeated content into reuse library.
- Metadata pass: keywords, attributes, taxonomy.
- Validate: run builds and resolve warnings/errors.
- Publish: produce HTML/PDF; confirm navigation and search behavior.
- Maintain: version and update with stable IDs and reuse targets.

text:
# Reuse, Keys, and Variant Management
## Reuse types: topic reuse vs content reuse vs reference reuse
For many organizations, multi-format output is helpful—but reuse is what changes the economics of documentation. Reuse reduces:

Topic reuse
You reuse a whole topic by including it in multiple maps. This is the cleanest form:
- least fragile,
- easiest to understand,
- best for long-term maintenance.
Example use cases
- a “Safety overview” concept used in multiple manuals,
- a “Reset password” task reused across product editions,
- a “CLI options” reference topic used in both admin and developer docs.
Content reuse (partial reuse)
You reuse parts of topics:
- warnings,
- short paragraphs,
- shared steps,
- reusable tables.
This is powerful but can create complexity if overused.
Reference reuse (keys)
You reuse references (links, product names, terms) using keys so the same topic can adapt to different contexts without editing.

# Reuse, Keys, and Variant Management ## Conref: direct content reuse For many organizations, multi-format output is helpful—but reuse is what changes the economics of documentation. Reuse reduces: What conref does conref allows an element to pull content from another location. Think of it as “include this snippet here.” Example ```xml

``` This pulls the element with id="install-note" from the topic with id="common-notes" in common-notes.dita. When conref is a great choice - stable legal text, - standardized safety notes, - common prerequisites, - consistent step fragments. When conref causes problems - when the reused content depends on local context, - when authors “fix” shared snippets without coordinating, - when the referenced snippet changes structure often, - when the conref chain becomes too deep to understand. Conref governance patterns (what professional teams do) Pattern A: Shared library ownership - Create a reuse/ folder (or CCMS equivalent). - Assign ownership to a small group. - Use review requirements for changes to shared snippets. Pattern C: Shallow reuse Avoid deep conref chains: - don’t conref a snippet that contains conrefs that contain conrefs… - shallow reuse is easier to debug and maintain.

text:
# Reuse, Keys, and Variant Management
## Conkeyref: content reuse through keys
For many organizations, multi-format output is helpful—but reuse is what changes the economics of documentation. Reuse reduces:

What conkeyref is
conkeyref lets you pull content via a key instead of a direct file#id reference. This is powerful when:
- the source of reusable content varies by deliverable,
- the same topic must pull different snippets depending on map context.
Example scenario
You publish the same “Install” topic for:
- Product A,
- Product B,
- Partner Edition.
Each needs a slightly different legal notice. Instead of editing the topic or maintaining three variants, you define:
- legal-install-note key in each map,
- the topic uses conkeyref="legal-install-note".
This is advanced, but it scales well when managed correctly.
Conkeyref example (complete)
Step 1 — Define the key in each map to point to a different snippet source:
```xml
<!-- product-a.ditamap -->
<keydef keys="legal-install-note" href="reuse/product-a-legal.dita"/>

<!-- product-b.ditamap -->
<keydef keys="legal-install-note" href="reuse/product-b-legal.dita"/>
```
Step 2 — Create the snippet sources with matching IDs:
```xml
<!-- reuse/product-a-legal.dita -->
<topic id="legal-notices">
  <title>Legal</title>
  <body>
    <note id="install-legal">
      Product A is licensed under the Acme Standard License.
      See https://acme.example.com/license-a for details.
    </note>
  </body>
</topic>
```
```xml
<!-- reuse/product-b-legal.dita -->
<topic id="legal-notices">
  <title>Legal</title>
  <body>
    <note id="install-legal">
      Product B is licensed under the Acme Enterprise License.
      Contact your account manager for terms.
    </note>
  </body>
</topic>
```
Step 3 — Use conkeyref in the shared topic:
```xml
<!-- topics/install.dita (shared across both maps) -->
<task id="install-task">
  <title>Installation</title>
  <taskbody>
    <prereq>
      <note conkeyref="legal-install-note/install-legal"/>
    </prereq>
    <!-- ... steps ... -->
  </taskbody>
</task>
```
When you build with product-a.ditamap, the conkeyref resolves to the Product A legal note. Build with product-b.ditamap, and the same topic automatically shows the Product B text — no editing required.
When conkeyref is worth the effort
- many branded deliverables,
- partner ecosystems,
- product families with shared content but different key spaces.

section_title: Filtering and variant management: one source, many outputs
text:
# Reuse, Keys, and Variant Management
## Filtering and variant management: one source, many outputs
For many organizations, multi-format output is helpful—but reuse is what changes the economics of documentation. Reuse reduces:

DITA filtering is often called profiling or conditional processing.
How filtering works
- You tag content with attributes:
- platform="windows"    - audience="admin"    - product="pro"
- Your build rules include/exclude based on those attributes.
Choosing attribute strategy (a big decision)
Common attribute dimensions
- platform: windows/linux/mac
- audience: user/admin/developer
- product/edition: standard/pro/enterprise
- region: us/eu/apac
- delivery: internal/external
Don’t use everything Pick only the dimensions you truly need. Each added dimension multiplies complexity.
Controlled vocabulary (non-negotiable at scale)
If authors invent values freely, filtering becomes unreliable:
- windows, Windows, win, win32 (all different!)
Professional programs define allowed values and enforce them.
Subject scheme maps (governance tool)
Many DITA ecosystems use subject scheme maps to:
- define allowed attribute values,
- provide taxonomy and classification,
- support consistent filtering.
Even if you don’t implement subject scheme immediately, write down your vocabulary and enforce it in reviews.

section_title: Designing reusable and filterable content without chaos
text:
# Reuse, Keys, and Variant Management
## Designing reusable and filterable content without chaos
For many organizations, multi-format output is helpful—but reuse is what changes the economics of documentation. Reuse reduces:

Here are strategies that prevent “variant spaghetti.”
Strategy A: Separate by topic when variants are large
If Windows and Linux procedures differ significantly:
- create separate task topics,
- assemble them differently per deliverable.
Use filtering only for minor differences, not completely different workflows.
Strategy B: Use filtering for small differences only
Filtering works well for:
- a few steps that differ,
- a paragraph that differs,
- a note that applies only to one platform.
If half the topic is conditional, split the topic.
Strategy C: Keep conditional blocks coarse
Instead of tagging every sentence:
- tag whole paragraphs,
- tag whole steps,
- tag whole sections.
This reduces maintenance.
Strategy D: Combine keys + filtering intelligently
Keys handle “context changes” (names/links). Filtering handles “content inclusion changes” (platform-specific steps). Use both; don’t force one mechanism to do everything.

section_title: Validation: how you keep reuse and variants reliable
text:
# Reuse, Keys, and Variant Management
## Validation: how you keep reuse and variants reliable
For many organizations, multi-format output is helpful—but reuse is what changes the economics of documentation. Reuse reduces:

A DITA program needs automated checks:
- broken conrefs,
- missing targets,
- unresolved keys,
- invalid attribute values (where possible),
- build warnings treated as failures in CI.
Practical quality gates
- Build must complete with zero unresolved references.
- Key space must resolve without ambiguity.
- Filtering must produce non-empty outputs for required deliverables.

# Reuse, Keys, and Variant Management
## A realistic “reuse plan” for a growing doc set
For many organizations, multi-format output is helpful—but reuse is what changes the economics of documentation. Reuse reduces:

- Keep topics portable across maps.
- Filtering for platform/audience
- Choose 1–2 dimensions; don’t overcomplicate.
Then scale:
- introduce key scopes if needed,
- introduce conkeyref for partner ecosystems,

Fix: define allowed values and enforce them.

Fix: use keys and keyref.

text:
# Technical Reference: XSLT Patterns and Glossary
## Quick glossary (developer-focused)
Most real DITA-OT customization work is XSLT. These patterns minimize upgrade pain and maximize maintainability.

- Transtype: the transform type, e.g., html5, pdf
- Preprocess / preprocess2: legacy vs map-first preprocessing pipelines
- Plug-in: extension package that can inject XSLT, Ant targets, resources
- Extension point: named hook where a plug-in can contribute behavior
- Intermediate files: normalized/filtered content used by later stages
- DITAVAL: XML file defining conditional processing (include/exclude) rules
- Chunking: how DITA-OT decides to split or combine topics into output pages/files
- @class attribute: inheritance chain used by XSLT to match specialized elements

topic_title: Adding Pipeline Steps: Pre/Post Preprocess and Legacy Hooks
section_title: The stable approach: run before/after preprocessing
text:
# Adding Pipeline Steps: Pre/Post Preprocess and Legacy Hooks
## The stable approach: run before/after preprocessing
How to inject custom Ant targets before or after preprocessing, and when legacy step-specific hooks are appropriate.

Use:
- depend.preprocess.pre
- depend.preprocess.post
Example plugin.xml snippet:
```xml
<plugin id="com.yourco.pipeline" version="1.0.0">
  <require plugin="org.dita.base"/>
  <feature extension="depend.preprocess.pre" file="integrator.xml"/>
</plugin>
```
Then in your Ant file (e.g., integrator.xml), define a target that runs before preprocess.

# Debugging Playbook
## Determine where the defect originates
Systematic approach to isolating DITA-OT build failures across the three stages: preprocessing, transformation, and packaging.

- Wrong content (missing steps, wrong links, unresolved keyrefs) → preprocessing issue
- Correct content, wrong markup → XSLT transform issue
- Correct HTML, missing CSS/JS/images → packaging/copy issue

section_title: If you suspect preprocess2 vs preprocess mismatch
text:
# Debugging Playbook
## If you suspect preprocess2 vs preprocess mismatch
Systematic approach to isolating DITA-OT build failures across the three stages: preprocessing, transformation, and packaging.

Check whether the transtype uses map-first preprocessing:
- HTML5 uses map-first preprocessing as of 4.2
- PDF/HTML Help use it since 3.0
If your extension points aren't firing, it may be because step-specific hooks aren't available in preprocess2.
Note: The official docs explicitly warn that the internal order of preprocessing steps can change between versions. If you're hooking inside preprocess, assume you must maintain it across OT upgrades.

Symptom

        Missing chunks/pages
          map structure, @chunk, or chunk decisions

section_title: Reproduce in Docker to isolate environment issues
text:
# Debugging Playbook
## Reproduce in Docker to isolate environment issues
Systematic approach to isolating DITA-OT build failures across the three stages: preprocessing, transformation, and packaging.

If a build fails only on CI or only on a coworker's machine, run the same build using a pinned Docker image:
```bash
docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/src ghcr.io/dita-ot/dita-ot:4.3.5 \
  -i /src/dita/maps/book.ditamap -f html5 -o /src/out/html5
```
This eliminates OS-level differences, Java version mismatches, and locally installed plugin conflicts.

text:
# Extension Points and Plug-In Fundamentals
## plugin.xml is the contract
DITA-OT plug-ins use extension points declared in plugin.xml to integrate changes into the toolkit without modifying core code.

DITA-OT provides a series of extension points used to integrate changes into core code. Extension points are defined in plugin.xml; when plug-ins are installed, the toolkit makes those extensions visible to the rest of the toolkit.
DITA-OT explains that plugin.xml controls all aspects of a plug-in, using predefined extension points to locate changes and integrate them into DITA-OT.
Also important for teams: DITA-OT provides a RELAX NG schema and describes validating plugin.xml with an xml-model processing instruction.

text:
# GitHub Actions: CI/CD Publishing and Workflow Examples
## PR checks: build HTML and upload as artifact
Production-ready GitHub Actions workflows for PR checks, multi-variant builds, GitHub Pages deployment, and release packaging using the official dita-ot-action.

.github/workflows/dita-pr.yml
```yaml
name: DITA PR build

on:
  pull_request:

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build HTML5
        uses: dita-ot/dita-ot-action@master
        with:
          input: dita/maps/book.ditamap
          transtype: html5
          output-path: out/html5

      - name: Upload HTML artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: html5
          path: out/html5
```
