section_title: Multi-variant builds: audience/platform filters as matrix
text:
# GitHub Actions: CI/CD Publishing and Workflow Examples
## Multi-variant builds: audience/platform filters as matrix
Production-ready GitHub Actions workflows for PR checks, multi-variant builds, GitHub Pages deployment, and release packaging using the official dita-ot-action.

.github/workflows/dita-variants.yml
```yaml
name: DITA variants

on:
  push:
    branches: [ "main" ]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        variant:
          - { name: web,    ditaval: build/ditaval/web.ditaval,    out: out/html5-web }
          - { name: admin,  ditaval: build/ditaval/admin.ditaval,  out: out/html5-admin }
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build ${{ matrix.variant.name }}
        uses: dita-ot/dita-ot-action@master
        with:
          build: |
            dita -i dita/maps/book.ditamap -f html5 -o ${{ matrix.variant.out }} \
              --propertyfile=build/html5.properties \
              --args.filter=${{ matrix.variant.ditaval }}

      - name: Upload artifacts
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: ${{ matrix.variant.name }}
          path: ${{ matrix.variant.out }}
```

section_title: Deploy HTML to GitHub Pages on main
text:
# GitHub Actions: CI/CD Publishing and Workflow Examples
## Deploy HTML to GitHub Pages on main
Production-ready GitHub Actions workflows for PR checks, multi-variant builds, GitHub Pages deployment, and release packaging using the official dita-ot-action.

.github/workflows/pages.yml
```yaml
name: Publish docs to Pages

on:
  push:
    branches: [ "main" ]

permissions:
  contents: read
  pages: write
  id-token: write

concurrency:
  group: "pages"
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build HTML5 site
        uses: dita-ot/dita-ot-action@master
        with:
          input: dita/maps/book.ditamap
          transtype: html5
          output-path: out/site

      - name: Upload Pages artifact
        uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
        with:
          path: out/site

  deploy:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: github-pages
      url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}
    steps:
      - id: deployment
        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4
```

```yaml
  group: "pages"
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build HTML5 site
        uses: dita-ot/dita-ot-action@master
        with:
          input: dita/maps/book.ditamap
          transtype: html5
          output-path: out/site

      - name: Upload Pages artifact
        uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
        with:
          path: out/site

  deploy:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: github-pages
      url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}
    steps:
      - id: deployment
        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4
```
shortdesc: Production-ready GitHub Actions workflows for PR checks, multi-variant builds, GitHub Pages deployment, and release packaging using the official dita-ot-action.
author: Extense LLC

text:
# Map-First Preprocessing (preprocess2) in 4.x
## Why map-first exists
DITA-OT's map-first preprocessing completes map-level resolution before topic processing, simplifying key/scope handling and enabling topic-level filtering optimization.

DITA-OT provides a map-first preprocessing approach as an alternative to the default preprocess. It provides the same functionality but changes the processing model: it completes most map processing first and only begins topic processing after map-level features are resolved.
Later DITA versions added many map-level features (keys, key scopes, etc.) that make it harder to reliably process topics before map features are resolved. The map-first model formalizes "process the map first," simplifies responsibilities, and can process only the topics actually referenced after filtering.

topic_title: How DITA-OT Is Built and Its Core Architecture
text:
# How DITA-OT Is Built and Its Core Architecture
## The mental model
DITA-OT implements a multi-stage, map-driven architecture where preprocessing dominates the complexity and the temp directory is the single source of truth for debugging.

DITA-OT implements a multi-stage, map-driven architecture. Each stage examines some or all of the content; some stages generate temporary files used later, while others generate final outputs. Most processing happens in a temporary working directory, and source files are never modified.
You can think of every transtype as:
```
inputs (map/topics)
   |
   |  preprocessing (common)
   v
normalized / resolved intermediates
   |
   |  format-specific processing (HTML5, PDF, etc.)
   v
output
```
This is the key to fast debugging: if the intermediate artifacts are wrong, downstream output will be wrong.

text:
# How DITA-OT Is Built and Its Core Architecture
## Stages, temp dir, and why preprocessing dominates
DITA-OT implements a multi-stage, map-driven architecture where preprocessing dominates the complexity and the temp directory is the single source of truth for debugging.

Stages are designed around responsibility boundaries
DITA-OT's processing structure is:
- map-first or mixed preprocessing (depending on pipeline)
- transformation steps for topics and maps
- resource copying and final assembly
The "hard" part is not HTML or PDF formatting—it's the DITA model itself: keys, scoping, references, reuse, filtering, chunking.
The temp directory is your truth source
Because DITA-OT does not modify sources and performs most work in a temp dir, you can debug by comparing:
- preprocessed topic versions (resolved conrefs/keyrefs)
- filtered versions (DITAVAL applied)
- chunked result lists (how the build decided to split output)
DITA-OT explicitly describes this temp-dir-based processing behavior.

# A Minimal Developer Plug-In Template
## plugin.xml (minimal)
A complete starter template for an HTML5 customization plug-in including plugin.xml, topic override, TOC override, and build commands.

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<plugin id="com.yourco.html5.custom">
  <require plugin="org.dita.html5"/>
  <feature extension="dita.xsl.html5" file="xsl/html5/override.xsl"/>
  <feature extension="dita.xsl.html5.toc" file="xsl/toc/toc-override.xsl"/>
</plugin>
```
Key points:
- id must be unique and follow reverse-domain naming.
- require ensures DITA-OT installs the HTML5 transtype first.
- Two extension points: dita.xsl.html5 (topic transform) and dita.xsl.html5.toc (navigation).

# A Minimal Developer Plug-In Template
## Install and build
A complete starter template for an HTML5 customization plug-in including plugin.xml, topic override, TOC override, and build commands.

```bash
# Copy plug-in into DITA-OT
cp -r plugins/com.yourco.html5.custom DITA-OT/plugins/

# Integrate
dita --install

# Build

  --nav-toc=full \
  --args.copycss=yes \
  --args.cssroot=css/ \
  --args.css=brand.css
```

text:
# Preprocessing Deep Dive
## Why preprocessing is structured as Ant targets
DITA-OT preprocessing is a set of Ant-target steps that resolve keys, conrefs, chunking, and links before any format-specific transform runs.

DITA-OT preprocessing is described as a set of steps that typically runs at the beginning of every transformation; each step corresponds to an Ant target, and the preprocess target calls the complete set.
Ant provides:
- ordering (depends chains)
- incremental injection points for plug-ins
- composable, declarative pipeline wiring (targets invoking Java or XSLT)

text:
# Preprocessing Deep Dive
## Common preprocessing responsibilities
DITA-OT preprocessing is a set of Ant-target steps that resolve keys, conrefs, chunking, and links before any format-specific transform runs.

A practical breakdown (you'll see these reflected in extension points and step names):
- clean-temp: initialize working directory
- copy-files / copy-image / copy-subsidiary: ensure resources are staged
- gen-list: create file lists / job metadata
- mapref / mappull / topicpull: map/topic "pull" logic (metadata, navtitle, etc.)
- keyref: resolve keys and references
- conref / conrefpush: resolve content reuse and push patterns
- coderef: resolve code snippets pulled in
- chunk: decide how topics are split (into pages or combined units)
- maplink: generate relationship tables / link metadata
- debug-filter: diagnostics related to filtering
- plus additional steps depending on pipeline and output
The development docs emphasize that these steps are targets in the Ant pipeline.

text:
# Preprocessing Deep Dive
## Where to hook preprocessing (best practice)
DITA-OT preprocessing is a set of Ant-target steps that resolve keys, conrefs, chunking, and links before any format-specific transform runs.

DITA-OT recommends using extension points that run before or after preprocessing, and warns against hooking into individual internal steps unless absolutely necessary.
Recommended hooks:
- depend.preprocess.pre — run a target before preprocessing
- depend.preprocess.post — run a target after preprocessing
Why:
- your plug-in stays compatible across versions
- you avoid being tied to fragile internal step order (which can change between releases)

## Legacy step hooks (use sparingly)
DITA-OT preprocessing is a set of Ant-target steps that resolve keys, conrefs, chunking, and links before any format-specific transform runs.

If you must hook inside preprocessing, DITA-OT documents legacy extension points such as:
- depend.preprocess.conref.pre
- depend.preprocess.keyref.pre
- depend.preprocess.chunk.pre
- etc.
But there's a strong caution: internal step order can change, so these should only be used when truly necessary.

text:
# Repository Layout and Build Variants
## Build variants with DITAVAL filtering
Recommended real-world repository structure for DITA projects and how to use DITAVAL filtering to produce audience/platform build variants.

DITAVAL files define conditional processing rules for attributes like audience, platform, or custom props.
Example build/ditaval/web.ditaval:
```xml
<val>
  <prop action="exclude" att="platform" val="print"/>
</val>
```
Build:
```bash
dita -i dita/maps/book.ditamap -f html5 -o out/html5-web --args.filter=build/ditaval/web.ditaval
```
Store common settings in build/html5.properties and override only filter + output per variant.

text:
# Writing Robust XSLT Customizations
## Recommended patterns
Recommended XSLT patterns for DITA-OT customization: import layers, @class ancestry matching, wrapper overrides, and consistent hooks.

Even though the full OT pipeline is complex, most real customization work is XSLT. These patterns minimize upgrade pain:
- Import the default transform — xsl:import the shipped XSL for your target output.
- Override templates by @class ancestry — match="*[contains(@class,' topic/p ')]" to work across specializations.
- Prefer wrapper overrides — Use xsl:next-match to keep default logic but add wrappers/classes.
- Add consistent hooks — classes and data-* attributes for styling and analytics.

text:
# Safe Customization Strategy: XSLT-Import Overrides
## Why this is the best default strategy
The recommended customization approach uses XSLT-import extension points so plug-ins override only what they need while remaining upgrade-safe.

DITA-OT supports XSLT-import extension points, where the plug-in installer adds an xsl:import statement to default code so your override becomes part of the normal build.
- No edits to toolkit core
- Minimal overrides (only what you need)
- Upgrade-safe: you import the shipped transform and override specific templates
